text_blocks.text: Seismologists can use the arrival times of
seismic waves in reverse to image the interior of the
Earth. Early advances in this field showed the existence of
a liquid outer core (where shear waves were not able to
propagate) and a dense solid inner core. These advances
led to the development of a layered model of the Earth,
with a crust and lithosphere on top, the mantle below
(separated within itself by seismic discontinuities at 410
and 660 kilometers), and the outer core and inner core
below that. More recently, seismologists have been able to
create detailed images of wave speeds inside the earth in
the same way a doctor images a body in a CT' scan.
These images have led to a much more detailed view of
the interior of the Earth, and have replaced the
simplified layered model with a much more dynamic
